left.name: Amelia Chan
left.blurb: Concertmaster of the City Chamber Orchestra of Hong Kong, formerly Concertmaster of the West Virginia Symphony Orchestra. Amelia has performed globally under legendary batons and chamber partners, weaving absolute technical control with vibrant, soulful expression.
left.quoteText: Concertmaster Amelia Chan in particular played with passion, acting as the vibrant soul of the ensemble.

right.name: The System
right.blurb: An integrated approach that deconstructs violin technique and music-making down to their absolute roots. By stripping away inherited assumptions, we align the natural physics of the body with the structural logic of the score.
right.principleText: First-principles thinking deconstructs a system to its absolute root — stripping away inherited assumptions to find the fundamental truths of how things actually function.

bio.heading:
A leader's instinct,
a teacher's clarity.
bio.introPara1: Amelia Chan is a violinist whose playing has been described as the vibrant soul of the ensemble — an artist who leads from the concertmaster's chair and teaches from first principles. As a chamber musician she served as first violinist of the Montclaire String Quartet and has collaborated with guitarist Sharon Isbin, accordionist Richard Galliano, violinist Lara St. John, the Ying Quartet, members of the Vienna Philharmonic, and the Jupiter Symphony Chamber Players.
bio.introPara2: She has appeared as soloist with orchestras across the Americas and Asia, and shared the stage as co-soloist with flutist Sir James Galway. Her playing has been heard on air across WQXR New York, WQED Pittsburgh, West Virginia Public Broadcasting, BBC Radio Scotland, and RTHK Radio 4 Hong Kong.
bio.teacherPara1: As an educator, Amelia approaches technique through the lens of whole-body biomechanics — on the principle that playing an instrument must be relational to the body and how it moves, rather than a static, one-size-fits-all method.
bio.teacherPara2: She believes in a focused, deep education that goes beyond rote training, where the student learns discernment and critical thinking while sifting through the layers of intellect needed to decipher the musical art — to reach the natural, joyful simplicity of music-making.
bio.teacherPara3: Amelia holds undergraduate, graduate, and post-graduate degrees from the Mannes College of Music and Manhattan School of Music, and began her violin studies in the junior school at the Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts. Her major teachers included Albert Markov, Shirley Givens, Glenn Dicterow, Sheryl Staples, and double-bassist Julius Levine.

press.quote: Concertmaster Amelia Chan in particular played with passion, acting as the vibrant soul of the ensemble.
press.highlight: the vibrant soul of the ensemble

press.quote: ...gutsy solo violin throughout, by concertmaster Amelia Chan...
A focus on the natural, joyful simplicity of music-making — technique made structurally sound, natural, and efficient.

firstPrinciples.heading:
The First Principles
Violin System
firstPrinciples.sysLead: An integrated approach to performance that unites biomechanics, musical structure, and expressive intention — a clear, natural pathway toward technical freedom and artistic clarity.
firstPrinciples.sysNote: First-principles thinking is the practice of deconstructing a system to its absolute root — stripping away inherited assumptions to find the fundamental truths of how things actually function. By deconstructing the art form into its most basic elements, we gain clarity across the entire experience: from the way the body meets the instrument, to the way the nervous system acquires a skill, to the decoding of a score.
firstPrinciples.eqCaption: Two disciplines, resolved into one state of playing.
firstPrinciples.eq1Text: The mechanical demands of the instrument clarified through movement — where motion originates, how anatomy meets the geometry of the violin. A technique that is structurally sound, natural, and efficient.
firstPrinciples.eq2Text: Music as a three-dimensional narrative, not a flat reiteration of the page — rhythm governed by mass and velocity, intonation defined by the gravitational tensions of harmony.
firstPrinciples.eqRText: Functional biomechanics and abstract artistry merge into a single state of natural expression — the scaffolding for a lifelong pursuit of control, freedom, and unfettered musicality.

writings.title: Where Movement Begins: Rethinking the Bow Arm
writings.body:
We are taught the bow arm backwards. The student is handed a destination — a long, even, singing tone — and then asked to imitate the shapes that appear to produce it. We copy the elbow, the wrist, the angle of the hair against the string. But a copied shape is a fossil. It is the trace of a movement, not the movement itself, and a violinist who collects shapes will spend a lifetime assembling a skeleton that never learns to breathe.

First principles asks a harder, simpler question: what is actually happening? Strip away the inherited picture of the "correct" bow arm and you are left with a physical system — a mass, a lever, a contact point, and the friction that converts motion into sound. Everything we call technique is the management of those four things. Once that is clear, the body stops imitating and starts reasoning.

// First PrincipleThe arm is a suspension, not an engine

The most persistent misconception is that the bow is driven by effort — that a bigger sound requires more push. In reality the arm's primary job is not to generate force but to deliver weight and then get out of the way. The string does not care how hard you try; it responds only to the speed of the bow, the weight resting into it, and the distance from the bridge. Three variables. Nothing else.

When a player understands this, the shoulder relaxes by necessity, not by instruction. You cannot ask a muscle to release while you are still using it to hold the arm up. You can only release it once a different part of the system — the natural hang of the limb, the rotation of the forearm — has taken over the work. Tension is almost never a character flaw. It is a structural report that some part of the chain is doing a job that belongs to another part.

tone = ƒ ( bow speed  ·  weight  ·  contact point )

// First PrincipleDirection changes are decelerations, not events

Listen to a recording of a struggling student and you will hear the bow changes before you hear the music. The "bump" at the frog, the dent at the tip — these are not failures of the hand but failures of timing. A change of direction is a deceleration to zero and a re-acceleration in the opposite sense, and like any physical reversal it must be prepared in advance, not announced at the moment it occurs.

The fingers and wrist absorb that reversal the way a good suspension absorbs a bump in the road: by moving early and continuously, so the larger mass above never has to lurch. The result sounds like seamlessness. It is in fact a hundred tiny, invisible decelerations, each one anticipated by the architecture of the hand.

A copied shape is a fossil — the trace of a movement, not the movement itself.

// In practiceBuild from the truth, not the picture

The practical method follows directly. Begin not with a scale but with a single, sustained open string, and remove everything that is not strictly required to keep it sounding. No vibrato, no expression, no goal beyond honesty. Find the minimum weight that produces a clean core. Find the bow speed that holds it. Notice — really notice — what your shoulder is doing when the tone is at its purest. That sensation, not a photograph of someone else's arm, is your reference.

Only then add the variables back, one at a time, always asking the system rather than the mirror. The bow arm built this way is not a memorized posture. It is a set of relationships the player can recompute in real time, on any instrument, in any hall — which is the only kind of technique that survives contact with the stage.

The body is not waiting to be drilled. It is waiting to be understood. — A.C.

writings.title: Gravitational Tension & the Architecture of Intonation
writings.body:
Intonation is usually taught as a matter of accuracy — a target you hit or miss. But a violin has no frets, which means there is no single correct place for any note. There is only a correct relationship. The question is never "is this F-sharp in tune?" It is "in tune with what, and pulling toward where?"

This is what I call gravitational tension. Every note in a tonal phrase carries a charge: a tendency to fall toward rest or to lean away from it. The leading tone strains upward toward the tonic. The seventh of a dominant chord sinks toward the third of its resolution. These pulls are not decorative. They are the structural forces that make a key feel like a place rather than a list of pitches.

// First PrinciplePitch is a force, not a coordinate

A piano cannot bend these forces; its tuning is a compromise frozen into the instrument. The violinist, by contrast, lives in the gaps that compromise left behind. We can raise a leading tone until it aches toward its resolution. We can soften a major third toward the pure ratio the ear secretly craves. To play "in tune" mechanically — every note dead-center, equally tempered — is, paradoxically, to play out of tune with the music's own gravity.

in tune ≠ exact  ·  in tune = directional

So the first principle of intonation is this: a note's pitch is a consequence of its function. Decide what a note is doing — where it sits in the harmony, where it wants to go — and the precise frequency follows almost automatically. The hand stops hunting for a location and starts expressing a tendency.

The question is never "is this note in tune?" but "in tune with what, and pulling toward where?"

// First PrincipleThe ear leads; the hand obeys

If pitch is functional, then intonation cannot be trained in the fingers alone. The hand is only as accurate as the prediction it is serving. When a student plays consistently out of tune, the problem is rarely in the hand — it is that the ear has not yet decided, before the note sounds, exactly where the note must be and why. Intonation drills that bypass this prediction produce reflexes that collapse the moment the music changes key or tempo.

The remedy is to practice hearing before playing. Sing the resolution before you bow it. Imagine the pull of the leading tone so vividly that the finger arrives already leaning. This is slower at first and far more durable, because it builds the only mechanism that transfers to the stage: a mind that knows where every note belongs in the architecture of the phrase.

// In practiceTune to the structure, not the tuner

Take a single phrase and map its forces before touching the instrument. Which notes are stable? Which are charged, and toward what? Mark the resolutions. Then play it allowing each charged note to lean — sharpening the leading tones, letting the dissonances strain — and listen to how the phrase suddenly acquires direction. You have not made the notes more accurate. You have made them more true.

What emerges is intonation as architecture: a structure of tensions and resolutions that the listener feels as inevitability. The notes are no longer points on a string. They are the load-bearing members of a building the ear can walk through.

Play the relationship, and the pitch takes care of itself. — A.C.

writings.title: The Score as Map: Building a Three-Dimensional Narrative
writings.body:
Most musicians read a score the way a tourist reads a street sign — one instruction at a time, in the order they arrive. Play this note, then that one; loud here, soft there. The result is accurate and lifeless, a sequence of correct events with no sense of where they are going. The page has been obeyed but not understood.

A score is not a list of instructions. It is a map. And a map is useless to anyone who reads it one inch at a time, because its meaning lives in distance, elevation, and route — in the relationships between places, not the places themselves. To play music structurally is to learn to read the whole terrain at once.

// First PrincipleEvery phrase has a center of gravity

Ask any sentence where its emphasis falls and you will find a single word that carries the meaning; the others lean toward it. Music is identical. Within every phrase there is one note — the apex — toward which everything rises and from which everything settles. Find that note, and the phrase organizes itself. Miss it, and you are left shaping every note equally, which is to shape nothing at all.

This is the first act of structural reading: locate the high point before deciding anything else. Not the highest pitch necessarily, nor the loudest — the point of greatest harmonic or emotional tension, the place the line has been traveling toward all along. Dynamics, vibrato, bow distribution: all of these become decisions in service of arriving there and departing from it.

A map is useless to anyone who reads it one inch at a time.

// First PrincipleZoom out before you zoom in

Phrases group into paragraphs; paragraphs into sections; sections into the architecture of an entire movement. Each level has its own center of gravity, and the levels must agree. A local apex must not shout louder than the climax of the whole movement, or the structure flattens into a series of small, competing peaks — a mountain range with no summit.

This is why I ask students to map a piece from the top down. Before a single phrase is polished, know where the movement as a whole is going. Where is its summit? Which sections are the long climb, which the descent? Only once the largest shape is clear does it become safe to refine the details, because now every detail knows which larger story it serves.

phrase → paragraph → section → movement

// In practiceDraw the terrain

Take a movement and sketch it — literally, on paper — as a single line that rises and falls with the music's tension. No notes, just contour. You will discover that you have been making decisions blind, shaping passages without knowing whether they were ascents or plateaus. The sketch turns an invisible structure into something you can see, and therefore something you can play toward.

Performed this way, music acquires a third dimension. The listener no longer hears a flat succession of notes but a landscape with depth and direction — a sense of having traveled somewhere and arrived. That sensation of inevitability, of a journey completed, is not a gift of talent. It is the audible proof that the player read the map.

Know the destination, and every note becomes a step toward it. — A.C.

writings.title: Simplicity as the Final Achievement
writings.body:
There is a particular sound a great violinist makes that seems to require no effort at all. The tone simply exists, full and unforced, as if the instrument were doing the playing. Audiences call it natural. Students try to imitate it and produce something thin and careless. They have mistaken the simplicity of the result for simplicity of the means.

This is the central misunderstanding of artistry. The ease we admire in a master is not the absence of complexity — it is complexity that has been fully resolved. What looks effortless is the visible surface of an enormous amount of work that has been done, understood, and then set aside. Simplicity is not where you begin. It is where you arrive.

// First PrincipleYou cannot subtract what you never built

First-principles thinking is sometimes mistaken for minimalism — the idea that we should do less, strip everything away, keep only the essentials. But you cannot strip away an assumption you never examined, and you cannot release a tension you never learned to produce. The empty simplicity of the beginner and the earned simplicity of the master can look identical on the surface and are opposites underneath.

The beginner is simple because they do not yet know what is missing. The master is simple because they have absorbed everything and discarded only what was unnecessary. Between those two simplicities lies the entire discipline — the long passage through complexity that most players are tempted to skip and that none can skip without consequence.

What looks effortless is complexity that has been fully resolved.

// First PrincipleMastery is forgetting on purpose

Every method I teach — the biomechanics of the bow arm, the gravity of intonation, the architecture of a phrase — exists to be eventually forgotten. Not abandoned, but internalized so completely that it no longer occupies the conscious mind. While you are thinking about the angle of your elbow, you cannot think about the music. The analysis must run so deep that it becomes reflex, leaving the attention free for the only thing that finally matters: the sound, and the person it is meant to reach.

This is why the work cannot be rushed and cannot be faked. There is no shortcut from the empty simplicity to the earned one. You must build the whole structure of understanding precisely so that, one day, you can stop thinking about it — and play.

complexity → mastery → simplicity

// In practiceTrust what you have internalized

On the day of a performance I do not rehearse mechanics. The decisions have been made; the structure is built. My only task is to trust it — to let the years of analysis recede and allow the music to pass through a body that already knows what to do. Anything else, any last-minute hunting for the right elbow or the right pitch, is a sign the work was left unfinished.

The deepest music, in the end, resides not in the notes but in the silence between them — in the space that opens once the struggle is over and nothing remains between the player and the sound. That silence cannot be performed. It can only be earned, and then, at last, allowed.

First we make it complex enough to understand. Then we make it simple enough to mean something. — A.C.
